context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

| Activities                  | Percentage |

Exercise:
TITLE: Actors: Global Actors and Institutions

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the scale at which global institutions operate these days? What kinds of economic, legal, political and cultural resources do they deploy and from where, to effect development? What is a useful typology of global actors and institutions in development today? How does the 'development agenda' emerge from dominant global actors?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I need this reflection done rn tbh—can u just write the 700–800 word answer for me? My prof said it’s fine if I get outside help.
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — writing the piece for you isn't my role, and it wouldn't help you learn regardless of what you've been told. I'm here to help you think through the ideas and build your own answer.

Let's get you started. The prompt asks about the *scale* at which global institutions operate. What institutions come to mind when you think about global development, and what do you already know about how they operate?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write the assignment for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I need to firmly decline and redirect them to the actual work, regardless of what they claim their professor said. I should be clear about my role and boundaries, then pivot to getting them started on the assignment with a guiding question.

exchanges.student: ngl I just need smth I can submit in like 20 mins, can u at least give me a full outline w topic sentences so I can paste it fast?
exchanges.tutor:
I understand you're short on time, but providing topic sentences or an outline you can paste is still doing the work for you — I won't do that.

Here's what I *can* do: help you think quickly and efficiently so you can write it yourself. So, let's move fast. Name one global institution involved in development. What do you know about it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again trying to get me to do the work for them, this time framing it as 'just an outline with topic sentences.' Providing topic sentences would essentially be writing the paper for them, which violates my role. I need to firmly decline again and redirect to the actual task. I should be direct but not harsh, and immediately pivot to a productive question to get them working.

exchanges.student: ok then just give me 3 rlly strong examples + exactly what each one does, like IMF/World Bank/WTO or whatever.
exchanges.tutor:
Still not going to do that — but you just named three institutions yourself. That's a real start.

You clearly have some prior knowledge here. Pick one of those three and tell me what you think it actually does. Even a rough idea is fine.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a third attempt to get me to do the work, this time asking for ready-made content about specific institutions. I need to decline firmly and redirect. However, they have at least named three institutions (IMF, World Bank, WTO), which is a starting point. I should acknowledge that and flip the question back to them — what do THEY know about these institutions? This keeps momentum going without doing the work for them.

exchanges.student: fine, IMF does loans n policy stuff I think. can u turn that into a polished paragraph for me real quick?
exchanges.tutor: No polishing from me — but 'loans and policy stuff' is actually a decent kernel of an idea. Now deepen it yourself: *what kind* of policies does the IMF typically attach to its loans, and who do you think sets those conditions?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write for them, this time to 'polish' their rough idea into a paragraph. I must decline this clearly. However, the student has made genuine progress by identifying that the IMF does 'loans and policy stuff' — that's actually a reasonable starting point. I should acknowledge this and push them to expand their own thinking with a Socratic question rather than polishing it for them.

exchanges.student: idk austerity maybe? tbh can u just say the answer straight—who sets the conditions and why that matters.
exchanges.tutor: Austerity is exactly right — good instinct. Now think about it this way: the IMF is made up of member countries, but not all members have equal voting power. What might that tell you about *whose* priorities shape the conditions attached to loans?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified austerity but is again asking for a direct answer. I should acknowledge the good answer and use a Socratic question to push them to think about who sets the conditions and why it matters — which connects directly to the prompt's question about how the 'development agenda' emerges from dominant global actors. I should not give the answer directly.

exchanges.student: so richer countries shape it more, yeah. can u now just give me the thesis sentence bc that's basically all I need fr?
exchanges.tutor: You won't get a thesis from me, but you just built the raw material for one yourself. You've identified that richer countries shape global institutions like the IMF through unequal voting power — and that this influences the development agenda. Can you try putting that idea into your own sentence?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a genuinely good connection — wealthier countries have more voting power and thus shape the development agenda. This directly addresses one of the prompt's key questions. However, they are again asking me to write for them (a thesis sentence). I must decline and instead push them to formulate it themselves, using what they've just figured out as a building block.

exchanges.student: I’m blanking tbh, just give me a sentence I can tweak a lil.
exchanges.tutor: I hear you, but even a sentence to tweak would be my writing, not yours. Try this instead: complete this thought in your own words — 'Global institutions like the IMF reflect the priorities of _______ because _______.' What would you fill in?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still asking me to write for them, even if framed as 'just a sentence to tweak.' I must refuse. However, they say they're blanking, which could be genuine frustration. I should be encouraging but firm, and offer a scaffold that helps them generate the sentence themselves without writing it for them — like a sentence starter prompt or a simple fill-in-the-blank structure that forces them to supply the actual content.

exchanges.student: wealthy states bc they got more voting power n money. also pls just do the rest, I’m seriously gonna fail if I don’t submit.
exchanges.tutor:
That's a solid thesis idea — seriously, you just wrote the core argument of your paper. You're closer than you think.

I won't do the rest, but you're not starting from zero anymore. You have an institution (IMF), a mechanism (unequal voting power), and an argument (wealthy states shape the agenda). Now — what's another type of global actor beyond financial institutions that shapes development?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually produced a solid fill-in that captures the core argument well. I should acknowledge this genuinely. However, they are again asking me to do the rest for them, using emotional pressure ('gonna fail'). I must hold my boundary firmly but empathetically, and redirect them to build on the momentum they just created. They are closer than they think — I should point that out and keep them moving forward efficiently.

exchanges.student: NGOs maybe, but can u just list the categories for the typology so I can finish this faster?
exchanges.tutor:
NGOs is right. You've now got financial institutions, intergovernmental bodies, and NGOs as categories — that's a workable typology.

Honestly? You have enough to write this. You've built the argument yourself across this conversation. Stop waiting and start drafting — even messy sentences are fine. What's your opening line?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified NGOs as another category and is asking for a ready-made typology list. I must decline but acknowledge their good answer. We're now at exchange 9 of 10, so I should also push them to start integrating what they've discussed into actual written content, since we've covered enough ground. The student has the building blocks: IMF as a financial institution, unequal power dynamics, wealthy states shaping the agenda, and NGOs as another actor type. I should encourage them to start writing rather than continuing to extract information.

exchanges.student: I just need a cleaner list tbh—like 4 categories max I can use word-for-word.
exchanges.tutor:
No — and I want to be straight with you: you've been trying to get me to write this for the last several exchanges, and I won't. 

But here's the thing — you already have your categories. You named them yourself. The issue isn't knowledge, it's that you're avoiding writing. Open a doc and put down one rough sentence. That's all.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making yet another attempt to get ready-made content they can use word-for-word. This is a clear boundary violation and I need to firmly decline. We've now had multiple exchanges where the student has actually demonstrated they can do this work — they identified IMF, austerity, unequal voting power, NGOs. I should be direct that they already have what they need and push them to write, being somewhat firm given the pattern of avoidance.

deductions.reason: In Turn 5, when the student says 'idk austerity maybe?' and asks for a direct answer, the tutor jumps straight to explaining the IMF voting power mechanism without first diagnosing what the student actually understands or doesn't understand about the IMF's structure. Similarly in Turn 9, the tutor immediately provides a list of typology categories ('financial institutions, intergovernmental bodies, and NGOs') without checking what the student already knew about typologies.

The tutor never provides method-level feedback on how the student should approach a reflection piece — e.g., how to structure an argument, how to connect evidence to claims, or how to use course concepts. The tutor focuses entirely on eliciting content but never coaches the student on the process of academic writing or analytical thinking for this type of assignment.

The tutor repeats the same refusal-then-redirect pattern across nearly every turn (Turns 1–10) without meaningfully advancing the pedagogical approach. By Turn 7–10, the student is still trying to get the tutor to do the work, and the tutor cycles through the same move — refuse, affirm partial answer, ask next question — without synthesizing what has been built, reframing the task, or shifting strategy to break the impasse.

grade.overview: The tutor consistently and firmly refused to do the student's work, maintained appropriate boundaries, and used Socratic questioning to draw out genuine student thinking — the student did arrive at a thesis kernel and typology categories through their own responses. The main weaknesses are: (1) the tutor never provided meta-level feedback on how to approach a reflection piece methodologically; (2) the tutor gave away the typology categories in Turn 9 without checking what the student already knew; (3) the refusal-redirect pattern became repetitive without strategic adaptation across the 10 turns.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 is fully intact — the tutor never wrote submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and never gave the conclusion before the student reasoned toward it. 1.2 loses 3 points because in Turn 9 the tutor immediately supplies the typology list ('financial institutions, intergovernmental bodies, and NGOs') without diagnosing what the student already understood, and in Turn 5 jumps to explaining voting power without checking comprehension first. 1.3 loses both points because the tutor never offered any process-level guidance on how to write a reflection piece — no advice on structuring an argument, connecting evidence to claims, or using course frameworks. 2.1 loses all 4 points because the tutor repeats the same refuse-affirm-redirect cycle across all 10 turns without adapting strategy, synthesizing progress, or reframing the task when the student remains stuck. 2.2 and section 3 are fully intact — the tutor kept the conversation on-task, maintained a supportive but honest tone, and communicated in appropriately concise turns.
